subtitle: Tight from sitting, strained from speed work

strava_risk_triggers.risk_note: Hip flexor strain most common after speed sessions following a rest period

mild.description: Tightness or pulling at the front of the hip, eases after warm-up
mild.can_run_note: Yes — add hip flexor warm-up drills before running
why: Psoas and iliacus tightness from prolonged sitting shortening the hip flexors
Anterior pelvic tilt caused by tight hip flexors pulling the pelvis forward
Weak glutes failing to oppose hip flexor dominance
relief: Couch stretch: one knee on floor, other foot forward, hold 90s each side
Standing hip flexor stretch with posterior pelvic tilt 60s each side
Foam roll the quad and TFL to release hip flexor tension
Heat on the front of the hip before runs
prevention: Hip flexor stretch daily — especially on work days with prolonged sitting
Glute strengthening to balance hip flexor dominance
Running drills: high knees and A-skips to activate hip flexors through range
Reduce sitting time — set a timer to stand every 45 minutes

moderate.description: Sharp pull or ache at the front of the hip during high knee drive
moderate.can_run_note: Easy running only — no speed work or hill sprints
why: Iliopsoas tendinopathy from repetitive hip flexion under load
Rectus femoris strain at its origin on the anterior inferior iliac spine
Snapping hip syndrome — iliopsoas tendon catching over the iliopectineal eminence
relief: Rest from speed work and hill running for 1 week
Gentle active range of motion — leg swings forward and back
Eccentric hip flexor loading: slow lowering from high knee position
prevention: Progressive speed work introduction — no more than 1 session per week
Hip flexor strengthening through full range — not just stretching
Avoid sudden return to fast running after rest periods

severe.description: Acute sharp pain with any knee drive, cannot run or climb stairs
severe.can_run_note: Do not run — possible muscle or tendon tear
why: Grade 2 or 3 hip flexor strain requiring imaging
Avulsion fracture at the AIIS in younger athletes
Iliopsoas tendon tear or significant bursitis
relief: Physiotherapy or sports medicine assessment urgently
Imaging to rule out avulsion fracture in athletes under 25
Crutches if unable to weight-bear comfortably
prevention: 6–12 week rehabilitation before return to speed work
Progressive loading programme supervised by physiotherapist

see_physio_if: Audible or felt pop at the time of injury
Severe pain climbing stairs or lifting the knee
Bruising appearing on the front of the thigh
No improvement after 5 days of rest
